structure.title: Preface
structure.summary: This preface section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document provides an overview and key metadata related to its creation and maintenance. It introduces essential concepts such as the AUTOSAR ownership and responsibility for the specification, along with versioning information and a detailed change history. The document covers raw data streaming interfaces using both IP-based protocols (network layer) and IEEE1722 protocol (data link layer), detailing security measures, use cases, and specific API classes and functions. Key technical components include various header files defining classes like `RawDataStreamClient`, `RawDataStreamServer`, and detailed fields within AVTPDU headers, which are crucial for understanding the data streaming processes and error handling mechanisms.

structure.title: 1 Introduction and functional overview
structure.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document introduces the functionality and API for processing raw binary data streams in the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. Key concepts include the distinction between byte stream and datagram stream handling, with support for both TCP/IP sockets over Ethernet using IP-based protocols and IEEE1722 protocol at the data link layer. The integration of these interfaces is defined during deployment through specific attributes and parameters. Secure communication and access control are also addressed, with TLS/IPSec for IP-based protocols and MACSec for IEEE1722.

structure.title: 2 Acronyms and Abbreviations
structure.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document provides a glossary of acronyms and abbreviations specific to this technical specification. Key concepts include network protocols such as IP, SOME/IP, TCP, UDP, and security protocols like TLS and DTLS. Additionally, it defines various formats related to audio-video transmission, including RVF (Raw Video Format), CRF (Control Reference Format), TSCF (Time Sensitive Control Format), and NTSCF (None Time Sensitive Control Format). The definitions highlight the specific roles of these terms within the context of data stream specifications, particularly in relation to end-to-end communication protection mechanisms.

nodes.title: 2.1.1 MAC unicast socket connection
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document explains the concept of a "MAC unicast socket connection," which involves communication through a socket bound to the data link layer (ISO OSI layer 2) using a media access control (MAC) address. This type of connection allows for direct, one-to-one communication with exactly one destination device. The key components include the socket provided by the operating system and the MAC address used for addressing. While no specific tables are mentioned in this excerpt, it emphasizes the unicast nature of the connection, ensuring that data is sent to a single recipient on the network.

nodes.title: 2.1.2 MAC multicast socket connection
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document discusses "MAC multicast socket connection," a method of communication using sockets at the data link layer (ISO OSI layer 2) that are bound to media access control (MAC) addresses. These connections enable reaching one or more communication partners simultaneously through multicast. Key concepts include the use of sockets provided by the operating system and the binding to MAC addresses, which facilitate efficient data transmission to multiple recipients in a network. While no specific tables are mentioned, this connection type is crucial for understanding how data streams can be efficiently distributed across a network using multicast protocols at the lower layers of the OSI model.

nodes.title: 2.1.3 AVTPDU-common-header
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document focuses on the AVTPDU-common-header, a fundamental component defined in IEEE1722. It consists of the first 12 bits of an AVTPDU-header and includes three key fields: subtype (8 bits), header specific (1 bit), and version (3 bits). These fields form the basis for all subtypes of AVTP stream data, providing essential information about the type of data being transmitted and its format. While no tables are explicitly mentioned in this excerpt, these fields play a crucial role in ensuring interoperability and correct interpretation of audio/video transport protocol data units across different systems.

nodes.title: 2.1.4 AVTPDU-common-stream-header
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document focuses on the "AVTPDU-common-stream-header," which is defined in IEEE1722 and builds upon the AVTPDU-common-header for specific types of AVTP stream data subtypes, such as 61883_IIDC, AAF, TSCF, and RVF. It introduces key concepts related to how this header structure enhances communication protocols within these subtypes. The document likely outlines important relationships between the headers used in different subtypes and specifies constraints or processes necessary for their effective implementation. While no tables are explicitly mentioned, technical details about the header's structure and functionality would be expected to provide a comprehensive understanding of its role in data stream specifications.

nodes.title: 2.1.5 AVTPDU-common-control-header
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document focuses on the "AVTPDU-common-control-header," which is an extension to the AVTPDU-common-header used for a subset of AVTP stream data subtypes, such as ADP. It introduces key concepts from IEEE1722, defining how this header expands upon existing headers to accommodate specific types of AVTP (Audio Video Transport Protocol) streams. The document likely outlines important relationships and constraints between these headers and the types of data they manage, though no tables are mentioned in the provided content.

nodes.title: 2.1.6 AVTPDU-alternative-header
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document discusses the "AVTPDU-alternative-header," a specific header type defined in IEEE1722. It is used for AVTP stream data subtypes that do not share common characteristics with those using the standard AVTPDU-common-stream-header or AVTPDU-common-controlheaders, such as CRF and NTSCF subtypes. The key concept here is that this alternative header allows for more tailored handling of certain types of audiovisual transport protocol data units (AVTPDUs) by providing a flexible structure that can accommodate unique requirements not covered by the common headers.

nodes.title: 2.1.7 AVTPDU-payload
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document focuses on the definition and characteristics of the AVTPDU-payload within an AVTPDU. Key concepts include its role as the second part of an AVTPDU, carrying data encoded in the header, such as audio samples for AAF-payloads. The relationship between the payload and the header is crucial, with the payload's content determined by the subtype specified in the header. Although no tables are mentioned, this section emphasizes the importance of understanding how different payloads carry specific types of data based on their encoding instructions in the AVTPDU-header.

nodes.title: 2.1.8 Stream data producer
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document focuses on defining and explaining the role of a "stream data producer," which is an essential component in Ethernet networks. It continuously generates data that is transmitted via streams, ultimately being received by one or multiple "stream data consumers." The term "talker" is used interchangeably with "stream data producer," highlighting its active role in initiating data transmission within the network. While no specific tables are mentioned, this section establishes foundational terminology and relationships critical for understanding data flow and network architecture.

nodes.title: 2.1.9 Stream data consumer
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document focuses on defining and explaining the concept of a "stream data consumer," which is an essential component in Ethernet networks. It continuously consumes data received via streams, with this term being synonymous to "listener." The section outlines the fundamental role of these consumers within the network architecture and establishes their relationship to data transmission processes. While no specific tables are mentioned, understanding the roles and interactions between stream data consumers and other network elements is crucial for implementing effective data handling systems.

nodes.title: 2.1.10 AVTP presentation time
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document defines the concept of AVTP presentation time, which is crucial for understanding timing in audio/video transmission protocols. It explains that AVTP presentation time refers to the gPTP (Grandmaster Precision Time Protocol) time at which designated data within an AVTPDU payload is transferred to a time-sensitive application. The key component here is the "avtp_timestamp" found in the AVTPDU-header of header format AVTPDU-common-stream-header, and it is calculated as the sum of the current global time and the maximum transit time. Importantly, this definition excludes any processing or format conversion times within the receiving application, as illustrated in [1, IEEE1722] figure 6 "Figure 6 - AVTP Timing Reference Planes".

nodes.title: 2.1.11 Max transit time
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document discusses the concept of "Max Transit Time," which is defined according to IEEE1722. It explains that this value represents the maximum time required for data to travel from a stream data producer (referred to as a talker) to a consumer (listener). The key process involves identifying the worst-case transit times across all possible paths and selecting a "Max Transit Time" that is at least equal to the longest of these worst-case scenarios. This ensures reliable data transmission by accounting for potential delays, thus maintaining synchronization between producers and consumers in the system.

nodes.title: 2.1.12 Media clock
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document focuses on defining and explaining the concept of a "Media clock." According to IEEE1722, a media clock is an entity that generates a precise rate, such as a hardware clock with a constant frequency like 48kHz. The media clock operates within a system provided by a media clock provider, highlighting its role in generating consistent timing for media data streams. While no tables are explicitly mentioned, the definition emphasizes the importance of accurate and reliable timekeeping for media processing tasks.

nodes.title: 2.1.13 Media clock provider
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document defines a key component in media streaming infrastructure—the "media clock provider." This end node in the network hosts and transmits an IEEE1722 CRF (clock reference format) stream to one or multiple consumers. The transmitted stream includes presentation timestamps that correlate with the media clock rate, ensuring synchronization across the network. While no tables are explicitly mentioned, this setup is crucial for maintaining accurate timing in multimedia applications.

nodes.title: 2.1.14 Media clock consumer
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document defines a "media clock consumer," which is an endpoint in the network that receives a CRF (clock reference format) stream from a media clock provider over IEEE1722. The primary function of the media clock consumer is to recover its internal media clock, typically through a phase-locked loop (PLL). This process ensures synchronization and accurate timing for media processing tasks. While no specific tables are mentioned in this excerpt, it highlights the importance of precise clock recovery mechanisms in networked media systems.

nodes.title: 2.1.15 ACF-stream
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document defines an "ACF-stream," which is a specific type of IEEE1722 stream that can be either time-synchronous (TSCF) or non-time-synchronous (NTSCF). These streams are used to transport encapsulated bus frames as ACF-messages within the AVTPDU-payload. The key concepts include the different subtypes of ACF-streams and their role in transmitting data, particularly focusing on how they handle time-synchronization for control formats. No tables are present in this section, but it emphasizes the relationship between ACF-streams, IEEE1722 streams, AVTPDU-payloads, and ACF-messages like ACF_CAN.

nodes.title: 2.1.16 ACF-message
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document defines an "ACF-message," which encapsulates bus frames according to specific types (e.g., CAN) as per IEEE1722 standards. Key components include the ACF-message-header and payload, with multiple ACF-messages potentially forming an ACF-payload within a single ACF-stream. Importantly, this section outlines how different ACF-message types can coexist in the same stream, facilitating diverse bus communication protocols under a unified framework.

nodes.title: 2.1.17 ACF-message-header
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document describes the structure and composition of an ACF-message-header, which is the initial segment of an ACF-message. It consists of 16 bits in total, with the first 7 bits defining the type of ACF-message (such as ACF_CAN or ACF_LIN, specified by IEEE1722), and the subsequent 9 bits indicating the length of the message payload that follows. This header is crucial for interpreting the type and size of the incoming data, facilitating proper decoding and processing of the raw data stream.

nodes.title: 2.1.18 ACF-message-payload
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Raw Data Stream" document focuses on defining the "ACF-message-payload," which is part of an ACF-message as per IEEE1722 standards. The payload carries data encoded in the ACF-message-header, such as a CAN2.0 frame for an ACF_CANpayload. This definition highlights the relationship between the header and the payload within an ACF-message structure, emphasizing that the payload contains specific types of data depending on the message type. While no tables are explicitly mentioned, this section provides crucial information about how data is structured and transmitted in ACF-messages.
